claims.text: The reason the United States has not fully adopted the metric system is the fault of pirates.

evidence.quote:
If you want to blame someone,
you got to blame pirates.
Let's find out why.

claims.rationale: Host's thesis/setup claim, half-tongue-in-cheek. Historically the single privateer interception of the 1793 metric artifacts was a minor factor; much larger reasons include entrenched US Customary practice, 19th-century political resistance, and industrial lobbying. The 'pirates caused it' framing is a narrative hook, not a serious causal attribution.

claims.text: In 1793, the United States needed standards for weights and measures because each state was doing its own thing.

evidence.quote:
The United States was brand spanking new and
it needed standards for weights and measures

claims.rationale: Historically accurate. Article I of the US Constitution (1789) explicitly authorized Congress to fix standards of weights and measures; state-level systems (Dutch in NY, English in several, French/Spanish influence in others) did create interstate commerce friction. Jefferson submitted his report on weights and measures to Congress in 1790.

claims.text: Secretary of State Thomas Jefferson was a Francophile intrigued by France's new decimal-based system of weights and measures.

evidence.quote:
The secretary of state at this time was Thomas
Jefferson

France was in
the middle of their own revolution and was
implementing a new decimal based
system of weights and measures.

claims.rationale: Jefferson was Secretary of State 1790-1793 and a documented Francophile (ambassador to France 1785-89). France adopted the decimal metric system during the Revolution (1791 Académie des Sciences proposal, 1795 legislated). Jefferson's own 1790 'Plan for Establishing Uniformity in the Coinage, Weights, and Measures' proposed a decimal system predating the formal French adoption.

claims.text: France dispatched scientist Joseph Dumais to the US with a one-kilogram copper weight called a grave and a one-metre scale.

evidence.quote:
French scientist Joseph Dumais was dispatched to
the US

a one
kilogram copper weight called a grave.
And a one metre scale number was on his way
to P

claims.rationale: This story is partially corroborated but with errors. The envoy was Joseph Dombey (not 'Dumais'), a French botanist, dispatched in 1793 by the National Convention carrying a provisional metric standard. The 'grave' was indeed the pre-1795 provisional name for what became the kilogram. However, provisional metric artifacts were bronze/platinum, not copper. Host's transcription of 'Dombey' as 'Dumais' is a pronunciation artifact.

dependencies.rationale: Result of Jefferson's interest in adopting the French system.

claims.text: Dombey's ship was blown off course in a storm and he ended up in the Caribbean where British privateer 'Teres' intercepted his ship and stole the metric artifacts.

evidence.quote:
It is in this case, British pirate Teres
intercepted a ship, stole th

claims.rationale: The Dombey interception is a documented historical incident — a British privateer captured his ship in 1794 in the Caribbean en route to Philadelphia. The privateer's name in historical sources is usually rendered as a ship/captain name — 'Teres' here is likely a garbled transcription of whatever name AJ said. Dombey did die in captivity (some sources: Montserrat). The broad story is real; name specifics are muddled.

dependencies.rationale: The interception only matters because Dombey was carrying the artifacts.

claims.text: Dombey died in prison and the metric doodads sold at auction.

evidence.quote:
he died in prison.
Oh, no. When the doodads sold at auction.

claims.rationale: Dombey's death in captivity is the generally reported fate — probably on Montserrat in 1794. The artifacts being sold at auction is less well-documented but plausible; standard prize-court practice of the era. The version that the metric standards failed to reach the US because of this is genuine history, not invented.

claims.text: By the mid-1800s the US had settled on the US customary system, which is based on the UK's imperial system.

evidence.quote:
US was pretty well settled on what's
called the US customary system, which is based
on UK's imperial system

claims.rationale: Partially accurate but with a temporal inversion. US Customary units were codified earlier, based on English units from colonial-era definitions; the British 'Imperial' system was legally defined by the Weights and Measures Act 1824 — meaning US Customary was not derived from Imperial but both descended from earlier English units. They diverged thereafter (e.g., gallon definitions differ). The host's framing is a common but incorrect simplification.

claims.text: In 1866 Congress authorized use of the metric system and supplied each state with a set of metric weights and measures.

evidence.quote:
1866, Congress authorized the use of the metric
system

claims.rationale: The Metric Act of 1866 (signed by President Andrew Johnson) did legalize the use of metric units in US commerce, making them non-prohibited but not mandatory. Standards were distributed to the states in the years following. Accurate.

claims.text: The US customary system is more accessible and relatable than metric because a foot is about the length of a human foot and an inch about the width of a thumb.

evidence.quote:
A foot is about the length of
your foot and inches about the width of your
thumb

claims.rationale: Pro-customary talking point. While customary units are indeed loosely anthropometric in origin, arguments for 'accessibility' are contested — metric is demonstrably easier for scientific and computational work, conversion, and trade. The 'relatable' framing is opinion-laden; the factual grounding (unit origin from body parts) is roughly true but doesn't establish a measurement-quality advantage.

claims.text: In 1875 the United States signed the Meter Convention, an international treaty creating a system of metric standards.

evidence.quote:
eighteen seventy five, the United States
signed the meter convention

claims.rationale: The Convention du Mètre (Treaty of the Metre) was signed 20 May 1875 by 17 states including the United States. It established the BIPM (International Bureau of Weights and Measures) and the formal metric system. Well-documented.

claims.text: American industrialists argued switching to metric would be too expensive and would harm American infrastructure.

evidence.quote:
Industrialists argued that switching to metric would
be too expensive

claims.rationale: Well-documented: US manufacturing associations lobbied against mandatory metrication throughout the 19th and 20th centuries citing retooling cost and infrastructure-disruption arguments. The American Metric Bureau's push in the 1870s-80s was countered by industry. Accurate political/economic history.

claims.text: There is no Illuminati.

evidence.quote:
You eggheads know it wasn't all NASA's fault.
You can also blame the
engineers at Lockheed Martin Illuminati.

claims.rationale: Host's meta-denial of the recurring Illuminati gag. The historical Bavarian Illuminati was suppressed in 1785; no credible evidence that a contemporary Illuminati organization exerts real-world influence. Well-established factual position.

claims.text: Officially in the US, a yard is not defined as three feet or 36 inches but as 0.9144 metres — customary units are defined by their metric equivalents.

evidence.quote:
a yard is not
defined as three feet or thirty six inches

A yard is zero point nine one
four four meters

claims.rationale: Correct. The 1959 International Yard and Pound Agreement fixed 1 yard = 0.9144 m exactly, defining US Customary (and Imperial) length units as metric derivatives. The pound was fixed as 0.45359237 kg. This is how all US Customary units are officially defined today.

claims.text: The UK switched to metric in 1965 because they wanted access to more European markets.

evidence.quote:
UK switched in nineteen sixty
five was because they wanted access to more
European markets

claims.rationale: The UK did announce a programme of metrication in 1965, and European trade integration (foundation of EEC, UK's ambitions to join) was a documented driver. But the UK is still only partially metricated — road signs remain in miles, pints are still legal units for beer. 'Switched in 1965' is a simplification of an incomplete multi-decade transition.

claims.text: In 1971 Congress recommended that the United States go metric within 10 years, but the country did not follow through.

evidence.quote:
in nineteen seventy one, Congress
recommended that the United States go metric

claims.rationale: The 1971 National Bureau of Standards report 'A Metric America: A Decision Whose Time Has Come' recommended a 10-year planned transition to metric. Congress did not pass binding legislation; the later 1975 Act was voluntary. Historically accurate.

claims.text: The Metric Conversion Act of 1975 encouraged metric use but did not require it.
entities: law_or_policy:metric conversion act of nineteen seventy five

evidence.quote:
Metric Conversion Act of
nineteen seventy five encouraged the use of
metric but didn't require it

claims.rationale: Public Law 94-168, the Metric Conversion Act of 1975, declared metric the 'preferred' system for US trade and commerce but made conversion purely voluntary. The Act created the US Metric Board (dissolved 1982). Accurate description.

claims.text: Big American companies like Xerox, GM, IBM, and Ford all switched to metric long before 1975.

evidence.quote:
big companies like Xerox, GM, IBM, Ford,
they all switched to metric

claims.rationale: Partially correct. IBM and Xerox did adopt metric internally for international products in the 1960s-70s. GM formally moved to metric in the late 1970s-early 1980s (around the X-body platform). Ford adopted metric in North American design during the 1980s. The 'long before 1975' claim is too strong for GM and Ford specifically but directionally correct for IBM/Xerox.

claims.text: NASA crashed the Mars Climate Orbiter into the Martian atmosphere because the orbiter's thrusters used metric Newtons while ground control used pounds.

evidence.quote:
NASA
crashed the Mars Climate Orbiter
into the Martian atmosphere.

thrusters on the orbiter were
being measured in metric Newtons, the ground
control was still using pounds

claims.rationale: Factually correct. The Mars Climate Orbiter was lost in September 1999 when Lockheed Martin ground software outputted thrust in pound-seconds while NASA JPL software expected SI newton-seconds, causing incorrect trajectory. The Mishap Investigation Board's 1999 report confirmed this unit-mismatch root cause. Canonical case study in unit-error engineering failures.

claims.text: The Mars Climate Orbiter failure vaporized $125 million that had taken years of work.
entities: quantity:one hundred and twenty five million dollars

evidence.quote:
one hundred and twenty five
million dollars vaporized because
they didn't use metric.

claims.rationale: The Mars Climate Orbiter mission cost is commonly cited as ~$125M (NASA figure for the orbiter itself; total Mars Surveyor '98 program with MCO+MPL was higher). Mission destroyed on MOI burn Sept 23, 1999. Figure is publicly reported and correct.

claims.text: During the 2016 presidential campaign, Lincoln Chafee made going metric a core part of his platform.

evidence.quote:
vision for having the United States
join the world by implementing metric.
That champion was Lincoln Chafee

Lincoln Chafee made going
metric a core part
of his presidential platform

claims.rationale: Lincoln Chafee, former Republican/Independent Senator and Democratic Governor of Rhode Island, entered the 2016 Democratic primary and did publicly advocate US metrication at his launch speech (June 3, 2015). It became a widely-mocked hallmark of his short-lived campaign (dropped out October 2015). Accurate.

claims.text: Today America is effectively a metric country: all federal agencies are required to use metric and all consumer and food products are required to have metric on packaging.

evidence.quote:
America is a metric country,
all federal agencies are required to use
metric

all consumer and food products are required
to have metric on the packaging

claims.rationale: Overstated. The Fair Packaging and Labeling Act (1992 amendment) does require dual (customary + metric) labeling on many consumer products. Executive Order 12770 (1991) directed federal agencies to use metric 'to the extent feasible' for procurement. But 'America is a metric country' overstates the reality: most everyday measurement (weather, road signs, cooking, sports) remains customary.

dependencies.rationale: [logical] The steelman 'customary is more accessible' premise conflicts with the implicit conclusion here that metric adoption is now the standard.
